title: 谋杀启事
summary:
Inspector Coladoke questions Miss Harris regarding Rudi Scherz, who she describes as a gentleman but suggests may be a fraud. She notes that while Scherz claims to be from a wealthy Swiss family, his clothing is inexpensive, and his stories of climbing the Alps contradict his tendency to feel dizzy on simple ridges. She denies knowing if he knows Miss Blacklock and is uncertain if he mentioned Chipping Clegg-horn, though he once inquired about bus schedules.

Coladoke arrives at a small, somewhat neglected estate to meet Chief Constable Fletcher. Fletcher reports that there were no signs of forced entry and that Scherz likely arrived by bus at six o'clock. While Miss Blacklock believes the front door is usually locked only at night, the maid claims it was locked all afternoon. Fletcher notes that the lighting system was manipulated, though they have not determined how the fuse box in the pantry was tampered with without being seen by the maid.

Upon entering, Coladoke is met by a young woman who informs him that Mickey has locked herself in her room and is making moaning sounds. Coladoke meets Miss Blacklock, an elegant woman with a bandage on her left ear, and her anxious companion, Dora Bunner. While surveying the Victorian living room, Coladoke notices a silver vase with wilted violets, which he considers out of place in the well-maintained house. Miss Bunner describes the chaos of the previous night, mentioning broken furniture and a cigarette burn. Miss Blacklock explains that she met Scherz three weeks prior at the Medenham Pleasure Gardens; he claimed to be the son of the owner of the Alpine Hotel in Monte Carlo, where she had stayed during the war.
